items.html:
Acute and chronic effects

Hazardous substances can affect health in two broad ways. An acute effect appears quickly after exposure, sometimes within minutes — think of stinging eyes, a headache, dizziness, a chemical burn or sudden breathlessness after a splash or breathing something in. A chronic effect builds up slowly over months or years of repeated contact, and may not be noticed until real damage has been done. Occupational asthma and some cancers are chronic effects. The very same substance can cause both, so neither should ever be dismissed as trivial.

Skin, lungs and who is most at risk

Two conditions are especially common in care work.

  • Dermatitis: inflamed, cracked or blistered skin, usually on the hands, caused by frequent contact with water, cleaning products and long spells in gloves — often called ‘wet work’.
  • Occupational asthma: the airways become sensitised to a substance, and once sensitised even tiny amounts can trigger an attack.

Some people are more vulnerable than others, including new and young workers, anyone who is pregnant or breastfeeding, and those with existing skin or breathing conditions. Report early signs such as red, itchy skin or a persistent cough, because acting early prevents lasting harm.

items.question: A colleague develops a wheeze and tightness in the chest that eases on days off but returns at work, after months of using one particular product. How is this effect best described?

answers.text: A chronic effect — it has built up over months and points to possible occupational asthma that should be reported.
answers.feedback: Correct. It has developed slowly with repeated exposure, and symptoms that ease away from work strongly suggest a work-related, chronic effect.

answers.text: An acute effect, because the symptoms are uncomfortable right now.
answers.feedback: Incorrect. Acute effects appear quickly after a single exposure; this has developed over months, which makes it chronic.

answers.text: Nothing to do with work, since everyone gets a cough from time to time.
answers.feedback: Incorrect. Symptoms that ease away from work and return on shift strongly suggest a work-related cause that needs reporting.

answers.text: A minor skin problem that will clear up on its own.
answers.feedback: Incorrect. These are breathing symptoms, not skin, and they should never be left to sort themselves out.

items.html:
Cleaning chemicals and dangerous mixes

Care settings hold many everyday products that become dangerous if misused. The most important rule is never to mix cleaning chemicals. Bleach mixed with ammonia — found in some glass and floor cleaners — gives off a toxic gas, and bleach mixed with acidic products such as limescale removers or toilet descalers releases chlorine gas. Both can seriously harm your lungs. Use one product at a time, follow the dilution instructions exactly, and make sure the area is well ventilated. Stronger is not safer, and more product does not mean a cleaner surface.

Body fluids, clinical waste, medicines and legionella

Other hazards in care are biological or medicinal. Blood, urine, faeces and vomit can carry infection, so treat all body fluids as potentially hazardous and follow your infection control and clinical waste procedures, using the correct colour-coded bags and bins. Some medicines are themselves hazardous substances — cytotoxic drugs, for example — and must be handled and disposed of exactly as instructed. Legionella bacteria are a quieter risk: they multiply in warm, stagnant water and can cause serious pneumonia if someone breathes in fine water droplets, for instance from a rarely used shower. Running taps and showers regularly and reporting water faults all help keep this risk under control.

items.taskDescription: Drag each word into the correct gap to complete the guidance on hazards in care.
items.textField: You must never mix bleach with *ammonia*, because together they give off a toxic gas. Bleach mixed with an *acidic* descaler releases chlorine gas instead. Treat all body *fluids* as potentially infectious and use the correct colour-coded *waste* bags. Legionella bacteria grow in warm, *stagnant* water and are breathed in as fine droplets.

items.html:
PPE as the last line of defence

Under the hierarchy of control, personal protective equipment (PPE) is the last line of defence, used only when other measures cannot remove the risk on their own. For hazardous substances that usually means gloves, an apron and, where there is a splash risk, eye protection. The PPE must be the right type for the job: disposable nitrile gloves resist many chemicals better than thin vinyl ones, for example. Check that it fits and is in good condition before use, wear it whenever your risk assessment says so, and remove and dispose of it correctly so you do not spread contamination to your skin or clothes.

Ventilation and RPE awareness

Controlling the air you breathe matters too. Good ventilation — opening a window or using extraction — lowers the concentration of fumes and vapours. Respiratory protective equipment (RPE), such as masks and respirators, protects the lungs where a real inhalation risk remains. RPE is not the same as a simple dust mask: to work, tight-fitting respirators must be the correct type for the substance and face-fit tested to the individual wearer, and they cannot seal properly over a beard. Most everyday care tasks do not call for RPE, but you should recognise when a task might, and always follow what the safety data sheet and COSHH assessment specify.

items.question: Your COSHH assessment lists disposable gloves and an apron for a cleaning task, with eye protection if splashing is likely. What does this tell you about PPE?

answers.text: PPE is a control to use as instructed on top of safer methods; wear exactly what the assessment specifies, in good condition, and dispose of it correctly.
answers.feedback: Correct. PPE is the last line of defence, and you should use precisely what the assessment and safety data sheet require.

answers.text: PPE removes the hazard, so once your gloves are on you can ignore the other precautions.
answers.feedback: Incorrect. PPE is the last line of defence and does not remove the hazard; safer working methods still matter.

answers.text: Any gloves will do, as long as your hands are covered.
answers.feedback: Incorrect. The type of glove matters; the assessment and safety data sheet specify what is suitable for the substance.

answers.text: Eye protection is optional and mainly for show.
answers.feedback: Incorrect. Where splashing is likely, eye protection guards against serious eye injury and must be worn.

items.html:
Decanting and labelling

How you use and store products is as important as the products themselves. Always keep substances in their original container with the label intact, because the label and safety data sheet tell you the hazards and the first aid. The golden rule is never to decant a chemical into an unlabelled container, and never into a drinks bottle or food tub — people have been seriously harmed drinking bleach or cleaner from a squash bottle. If you genuinely must transfer a product, use a proper container and label it clearly with the contents and their hazards. Close lids fully, and use older stock first so nothing is left to degrade.

Storage rules

Safe storage follows the safety data sheet. As a general rule, keep hazardous substances in a cool, dry, well-ventilated place, away from food, out of the reach of the people you support and anyone who might misuse them, and locked away where your assessment requires it. Store containers upright so they cannot leak, and keep incompatible chemicals apart — bleach must never be stored where it could mix with acids or ammonia if a container failed. Do not stockpile more than you need. Good storage prevents spills, slows deterioration and keeps everyone in the home safer.

items.question: If a spray bottle runs out, it is fine to top it up by pouring cleaner from a larger bottle into an old, unlabelled drinks bottle to carry it around.
items.correctMsg: Correct — this is false. Chemicals must never be put into unlabelled containers or drinks bottles, because someone could mistake the contents for a drink and be seriously harmed; always use a properly labelled container.
items.wrongMsg: Not quite — this is false. Decanting cleaner into an unlabelled drinks bottle is dangerous, as it can be mistaken for a drink; only ever use a proper, clearly labelled container.

items.html:
First response to exposure

If someone is exposed to a hazardous substance, act quickly and calmly, and follow the first aid on the safety data sheet.

  • Skin: remove any contaminated clothing and rinse the area with plenty of water.
  • Eyes: rinse gently with clean water for at least ten to fifteen minutes and get medical help.
  • Breathed in: move the person to fresh air.
  • Swallowed: do not make them vomit unless the safety data sheet says so, and ring 111 or 999.

Keep the product label or safety data sheet with you so medical staff know exactly what they are dealing with.

Health surveillance awareness

Some exposures are managed over the longer term through health surveillance. This is a system of regular checks — such as skin inspections or lung function tests — that COSHH requires where workers face an ongoing risk of harm, for example a risk of dermatitis or occupational asthma. It is there to catch early signs before they become serious, and to check that the control measures are actually working. Take part when it is offered, and always report symptoms yourself rather than waiting; you know your own body best. Reporting protects you and helps your employer improve the controls for everyone.

items.question: You knock over an open bottle of drain cleaner and some splashes into a colleague’s eye. What is the best immediate response?

answers.text: Rinse the eye gently with clean water for at least ten to fifteen minutes, get medical help, and keep the product label to hand for staff.
answers.feedback: Correct. Prolonged rinsing with water and prompt medical help give the best outcome, and the label tells staff what the chemical is.

answers.text: Tell them to rub the eye hard and blink until the stinging stops.
answers.feedback: Incorrect. Rubbing can grind the chemical in and cause more damage; the eye should be flushed with water instead.

answers.text: Wait to see whether it gets worse before doing anything.
answers.feedback: Incorrect. Chemical eye injuries need immediate, prolonged rinsing; any delay can cause permanent harm.

answers.text: Put a dry dressing over the eye and carry on with the shift.
answers.feedback: Incorrect. A dry dressing does nothing to remove the chemical; the eye must be flushed with water and medical help sought.
